speaker: interviewer
text: OK. It is Sunday, April 9, 1995. The survivor is Adela Manheimer. The interviewer is Masha Loen. The city is Los Angeles, California, United States of America. And the interview is being done in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Masha Loen, L-O-E-N. I'm conducting the interview with Adela Manheimer in Los Angeles, California. And the date is April 9, 1995. Thank you. Adela, I want you to tell me your life story. I want you to tell me from the beginning to the end-- till today, not to the end, because the end is far away. Just tell me where you were born, and let's start with your childhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I was a born in, in a little town in Poland. And it's Dabrowa Gornicza. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I was one child to my parents. My father happened to be an orphan when he was five years old. In a little town, an aunt raised him. And for this reason, everything was really emotional, because whatever he did, he realized and he appreciate his life and, and being raised for my aunt. So he became a philanthropist. His whole life he was working for poor and collecting money and doing everything what is possible for the poor, for to get married of and get us what didn't have-- poor. And, and he was the cashier from the Bikur Cholim, which was our organization for sick. And he was-- he had never have time for himself because when he went to synagogue, he always brought for my mother the poor people to take care of, to sleep over, and to fed them. And that's what he's always told me as a little girl, that you cannot take anything to your grave but your name. So he was living up to this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother struggled a lot about this, because some of them wasn't as clean or put a cigarette in the fire in our house or tried to take something. And we have a lot of-- as a child what was my observation. And then I get older. And I was one child. I, I really-- What was the profession of your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father start with tailoring. And then in, in my time, he was selling materials, like fabric, fabric.

speaker: interviewer
text: Adela, can did you tell us the, the month and day and year that you were born, darling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was born in, in June 21st, 21st, the first day of summer, 1921.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: The attention what I had from my parents was doubled, because [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my parents lost in '17 or '16 or I think '13 and, and '14 a daughter and a son before me, in one day. It was like here-- no problem in America. Was diphtheria, what needs the penicillin. And there was no cure in Poland this time so, one caught from the other. And-- and-- and 10 years later, I was born. So my father was a religious person. And he always went to the rabbi. And the rabbi gave him all instruction how to raise me, what kind name. And I was seven years wearing white, white, white. No other colors. I was wearing all kind of superstitious [INAUDIBLE] all kind of things, seven years to my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does the-- what did the meaning of white mean? Purity?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what the rabbi prescribed, like I got the prescription. And that's what my parents obey. After seven years, I was permitted to put a dress with a little colored polka dots. That was step by step, gradually. And then they decide to give me the best Jewish education. But being a Jew in Poland, you couldn't have, really, the higher education. It was impossible. So I reached to a bookkeeping school, private.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do before bookkeeping school? Did you go to public?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, I was public schooled. And then I went private courses to Bedzin, to another city, for bookkeeping. And I was going to a very orthodox Jewish school, like Bais Yaakov, Bais Yaakov, and-- and-- to become a teacher. But because I was one child, my mother couldn't take it that I should study in a different town, not to see me. And for this reason, I stopped this. But while I was growing up, I had very good atmosphere of, of friends, very nice surrounding. And I, I can say that I was lucky till the time came that the World, the Second World War broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before the war broke out--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war broke out--

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I want--

speaker: interviewee
text: -- I understood that I helped my parents in any direction in the house, in the profession.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you, did you feel any-- when you went to bookkeeping school--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you go-- did you-- were there Polish kids, too? Were there just Jewish, or it was mixed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was, it was the most-- because the Bedzin city contained from just the most Jewish people. And I, I cannot recall that I was anything. But when I was in public school, I had many very disappointments. When I was little girl, when it comes Passover, then you have new clothes, which was a custom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And non-Jewish kids passed by and spit at me or throw me a stone. When I went to the religious school beside the, the main city, I had thrown a stone covered with snow in my hat. I was very depressed. The last days before the war broke out was impossible, was-- the anti-Semitism was rising in Poland very badly. And our life was in, really, jeopardy because they were breaking the windows of the stores, of the Jewish stores. And it was impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we are-- we were very used to a hard life, considering-- like, Holland or other countries, Belgium, when came to our concentration camps. We were used to. So we are the one, we, we could take more than the others. The others usually, they failed after a few weeks. They were dying out. The nature of Poland Jews was very-- willpower. A strong willpower we had. Willpower to live.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Born willpower.

speaker: interviewer
text: Born willpower.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But me, as, as I was a one child, was really hard when I was taking-- I was working for the Wehrmacht, for the German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, let's see.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: The war started. How did, how did you find out that the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. We were-- when the war start, we looked out the window. And we saw the synagogue is burning. So the Germans came in. And they just burned the synagogues. They burn everything is possible and we felt. So we knew that the things just doesn't look good.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and I was not concerned about my life. I was this time, like, 17, 16. I was just looking at my parents. I couldn't believe it, what they're going through. What's going to happen with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father ran away to another city. He was miraculous survived. Because all the other people was thrown in the water. And he, he was survived by cutting off his hair. And he came back, but in a short time later, they were taking to-- this was not in my time. Then I went to the concentration camp. And they took me. And I was supposed to be protected.

speaker: interviewer
text: Weren't you first in a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was home. And I was protected working for Rosen. This was like Schindler, a factory what makes uniforms for the Wehrmacht, for the, for the German soldiers. So we thought that we not going to be taken in. So we were so sure, but one Saturday--

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you something. When you worked in that factory at Rosen--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you go home and come back to the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not put in no ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were just going home, work in the factory, and go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. After--

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no food. I was still home. But, but one Saturday, they made-- they came in, and everybody was taken to a [NON-ENGLISH] to another city, so Sosnowiec. And from there they send us to, to Grunberg in Schlesien, which was Germany already.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they send you?

speaker: interviewee
text: By, by force, by the Gestapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. They ask you to, to go, but how did they send you? Did you go by foot? Did you go by truck? How did they send you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, probably by trucks. I remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not probably. Tell me. Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I remember they took us--

speaker: interviewer
text: But they have cattle cars or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: --by train, by, by-- I, I don't remember exactly how they took us, because it was a time that the emotion, stress, and, and-- all young girls. We were just starting life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they took you by yourself without your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And my parents were still home. But when we were working to go there to this, I remember my mother running after us and, and, and handing me a piece of cheesecake. And then the SS man, I saw how he beat her up very badly. She was falling. And then she was spending all the time-- was January, was wintertime. Was standing down, down there to look up. And from all of this, she got pneumonia. She was very sick. Because I was already in concentration camp when other people came and told me this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which concentration camp was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grunberg Schlesien by Breslau.

speaker: interviewer
text: By Breslau.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they tell you there, the people, that your mother died or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My mother was taken to Auschwitz. She was 55. My father was, my father was 55. My mother, 50. They were taken to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which year were they taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all I know. That was probably [BACKGROUND NOISE] a year after I was taken. A year, probably, after I was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you taken? Which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were taken to a ghetto first.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were taken to a ghetto first, your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't there. That was, I think, '40, '41, '42. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: In '42, they took you to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: --to the concentration camp, and they put them into the ghetto. Was it a Bedzin ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, yeah. Between Dabrowa and Bedzin was very, very short distance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] And all-- in the beginning, my-- when I was in-- my parents still send me once in a while a piece of bread. But it never reached me. Because it was taken away or it was already stale.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know they sent it to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know, I know because the [NON-ENGLISH] what was leading us and was chosen to, to take care of us was a neighbor of, of ours. So my, my parents went to the sister what she lives with, and, and they give her gold and whatever that she is good to me, that she gives me something, but it never, it never, it never worked. Never worked. I was very, very depressed. So one time, an SS man from our camp came out and having-- to have fun. What he did, he, he told us, who has complaints to come out and say. And I was very naive. [INTERPOSING VOICES] And I came, I came into concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he ask you?

speaker: interviewee
text: If we have complaints.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, complaints. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just for fun. So I said, just for the sake that I am one child to my parents, suffered so much-- I, I was too naive-- I would like to, to get out. So I was beaten up. And I was two weeks laying almost dead from-- swollen and black and blue. And that was the fun of it. They were laughing and making fun. And I was a-- Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Adela, were there a lot of people, a lot of women--

speaker: interviewer
text: --a lot of people in the camp, 1,000 women.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just women.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just women.

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the last years came in few men--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to another--

speaker: interviewer
text: But in the beginning, it was just-- and when they beat you up, it was just women.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just women. We, we have one room, big, big, and we have three bed, one on the other. And we were working a night shift or day shift. We were making material for blankets for the Wehrmacht, for the soldiers. We learned this profession in six weeks, which other German women, it took two or three years. So he was very upset about it, this SS man. And he said, [NON-ENGLISH] how come the Jewish are so-- can, can--

speaker: interviewer
text: Smart.

speaker: interviewee
text: --learn so fast? He was outrageous mad. And for this reason, he came every Sunday. He put us in a line. And every 10th which came out had to go to Auschwitz to the, to the gas chamber. Not one time I was the ninth or the 11th.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that he was taking them to Auschwitz? Did they say that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was no secret. No secret.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were no secrets.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. One, one time we had a lot of vance, that was little bugs would--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bedbugs.

speaker: interviewee
text: --eat us up alive. Yeah. So, so one time one of this came in in mine ear. And I hardly could work because this was very precision work to weave on machines, material. So I complained to the nurse, was a German nurse, and I said that I have something in my ear, and it's jumping a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she went to the SS man. And she said that I'm foul, that I don't want to work. So he had a big ring and he beat me up. So I had also very swollen eyes, and I was all beat up because I didn't want to work, that's what they said. But after many years, many days, God was good to me. They took me to the [NON-ENGLISH], to the-- To the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they put some water on it, and came out a live vance. And I was relieved.

speaker: interviewer
text: A live bedbug. It was still alive in your ear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. For many days. And it was buzzing me. It was making me crazy. They didn't believe it. Because she was very, very mean, this nurse. This was all kind of things what happened to me while I was there beside the hunger, beside the dirt.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the food they gave you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the food-- they, they give a piece of bread, which maybe was three small slices, a little piece of margarine, and a soup what contains sand and, and you couldn't find a piece of potato. You have to look. So we always took out the piece of potato and left in a little dish for later to make a big, a big dinner from this. This was all what we had in the daytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you clean yourself? Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: The clean was very bad. We never had warm water. It was five years. Never warm water and not soap. The soap was like, like, what you-- [INAUDIBLE] something-- made from I don't know what. Was very bad. And that's all. I don't know how. I don't know. I was there. I lived through. And I can't believe myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, what kind of clothes did you wear there? Did you have striped clothes? Did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we didn't have special striped clothes, but I don't remember. I really, I really don't remember the clothes. I remember whatever we had, what we brought from the house, we, we had. And after so many years of being just on this diet what I told you, I really cannot figure out how can a person live like this? Because we, we didn't have any civilities to-- to-- to normal people, just like animals. Just animals. But somehow, God was with us. That's-- from 1,000 girls, maybe 80 survived. And from this 80, is just few left what I'm going to meet now in Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me-- did they-- tell me a day in the camp. What did, what did they do in the morning? You got up. You know, just tell me how the day--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right to the factory, was taking us to the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: We work-- yeah. We work almost 12 hours or something like this. We had to work on, on a special clock what has to do as many, as many--

speaker: interviewer
text: Pieces.

speaker: interviewee
text: --pieces. And, and if the, the thread didn't go and was turning too much, we were in trouble. Because if you don't make this, you have the possibility to be sent to Auschwitz. Everything was Auschwitz, Auschwitz, Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody was killed there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Some people were a little sick, so they send them to Auschwitz. They didn't bother anything. Just, if you're sick, you go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it-- was the camp close to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whole Poland was not too big. Was close.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was close from Grunberg to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Grunberg to Auschwitz was close.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And when a lot-- and some of them passed away, and some of them commit suicide, like put their heads down under the machine to finish. Not everybody had the willpower to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this camp, this factory, was part of Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a part of Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Department, yeah. Belonged to Auschwitz. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a department of Auschwitz. And name was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Grunberg Schlesien.

speaker: interviewer
text: Grunberg Schlesien.

speaker: interviewee
text: By Breslau. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was very hard. In the beginning, we, we received a few cards, mail, and then everything-- they, they, our parents was, all of them, sent to Auschwitz, and finito. Was finished. So we lived day by day and not too much hope to survive. We were-- we knew we cannot survive this. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somehow we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the only camp that you were in was that camp? They take you direct to this camp. And you were there from the beginning to the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was-- OK, I was there in beginning till the end, until January '45. In January of '45, they, they looked at themselves, and they decided that it's too close to-- it's going to happen, something. And they decide to take us out and to, and to let us go with, with their-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so they were on both sides of women and men, SS, and we were walking. But we couldn't carry our big belongings, so we throw out, slowly, slowly, and we were left with just one thing. And, and we approached a few countries. That was winter without shoes. I can't believe it. Now people get colds. We didn't get-- I didn't get a cold, even. I was no shoes in snow, no food. We were eating the snow on the way to-- I don't know. When it came to night, they put us in a, a stable from, from the cows and not let--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was good. That was warm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And not let-- not all the time-- not let us in to see there was machines. We fell on, on on the points machines. One or the other screaming, was all languages-- Hungarian, and, and, and it was just terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean to tell me that this was at the march--

speaker: interviewee
text: March.

speaker: interviewer
text: Death march. And you met up with other camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was all ours.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was all your people. And there were Hungarians there.

speaker: interviewee
text: The last-- the last, the last few months, they brought some Hungarians. But all the time was just the Polish neighborhood, like Bedzin, Sosnowiec, Katowice, and all them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. So we were walking. And every day was less and less. And they want to finish up that they can be free from, from their duties. But somehow, they, they shoot a lot of them. We had one time, I couldn't walk anymore. My feet were swollen. So I decide, whatever will be. I saw-- I'm petite. And I can fit in a doghouse.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the, in the light, in the morning, I decide I will run out to this doghouse. And, and, and when he catch me, of course, he can shoot me. So as a petite, I went into this doghouse.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of a sudden I see the, the [NON-ENGLISH]. He was a tall guy, SS man, with a small, a this small golden gun. And he was shooting everybody. So when I saw him coming, I said shema yisrael, and that's it. And he said to me, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. He couldn't believe it, what I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain it in English--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what he--

speaker: interviewee
text: Why did you run out in, in the light, in the daytime? Couldn't you do this in the night, to, to run away from me? He, he couldn't digest that, that I was so fresh, so, so impossible, bad. So I, I said my prayer, and I wait till he shoot me. I have nothing to lose. So he, he said, all of a sudden, to, to run after the march. This was--

speaker: interviewer
text: After the other people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This was because my father was-- he did so much good, I-- I-- I survive. I believe in this, that my father's--

speaker: interviewer
text: If you believe in it, it's a wonderful thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Keep on believing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So anyway, I, I run after this. And for being so excited, I had a little pillow or something in my hand that was my belonging. So I dropped it. And after maybe 10 minutes, I see him coming to, to the end of this march. I was the last one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And approaching me-- I said, now he's going to shoot me. You know what? He gave me this pillow. This is such a miracle. I can never forget this, as long as I live. He shot, in my eyes, a lot of girls with his little golden gun. And he didn't shoot me when he caught me running away, escaping. How did you do exist with food on the march?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. This is the question which I don't know. Sometimes we found an old bone or a old piece of beets on the way when we marched. We put it here. And we lick and lick. And from the snow. But one time we had a party. We went in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went in, in a, in a, in a stable. And the French people were there before. And they had baked potatoes, whatever, cooked potatoes. And this, the pail was under straw. So we had a party. Everybody was eating this thing. And then they became typhus, typhus, bloody typhus from all this cold, old potatoes. And this was the only party we had through the five months. So I like to make-- you have some questions. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I don't have no questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So--
